column4: Semiconductor diode formed by joining p-type and n-type regions, enabling rectification and later optoelectronic devices.
Fabrication method using silicon dioxide masking and surface passivation to make reliable, mass-producible silicon devices.
Field-effect transistors controlled through an insulated gate, becoming the dominant switching device for dense digital integrated circuits.
Complementary MOS circuits using paired n-channel and p-channel MOSFETs for low-power digital logic.
Light-sensitive polymer systems that define fine circuit patterns during semiconductor lithography.
High-precision patterned masks or reticles used to project circuit layouts onto photoresist-coated wafers.
Semiconductor doping method that accelerates ions into wafers to precisely control transistor electrical properties.
Dry etching process that uses reactive plasmas to remove selected thin-film materials in microfabrication.
Thin-film deposition process that forms solid materials on wafers from reactive gases.
Self-limiting thin-film deposition technique that builds materials one atomic-scale layer at a time.
Wafer polishing process combining slurry chemistry and mechanical abrasion to flatten multilayer integrated circuits.
Deep-ultraviolet projection lithography using steppers and scanners to pattern advanced semiconductor layers.
Extreme-ultraviolet lithography using 13.5 nm light to print the smallest high-volume chip features.
Software tools for specifying, simulating, verifying, placing, routing, and checking integrated-circuit designs.
Languages such as Verilog and VHDL for describing, simulating, and synthesizing digital hardware.
Methodology for designing very-large-scale integrated circuits with thousands to billions of transistors.
Processor architectures using simplified instruction sets, pipelining, and compiler support for high-performance chips.
Integrated circuit that combines processor cores, memory controllers, interfaces, accelerators, and peripherals on one die.
Dynamic random-access memory storing bits as charge in capacitor cells refreshed by control circuitry.
Static memory built from transistor latches for fast on-chip caches and buffers.
Generations of semiconductor manufacturing defined by device architecture, density, lithography, interconnect, and process design rules.
Three-dimensional field-effect transistors with fin-shaped channels that improve electrostatic control at small process nodes.
Transistors whose gates wrap around stacked nanosheet channels for continued logic scaling beyond FinFETs.
Chip fabrication approach routing power through the back of the wafer to reduce front-side interconnect congestion and voltage droop.
Next-generation EUV lithography with higher numerical-aperture optics for smaller features and reduced multipatterning.
Integration of multiple dies with interposers, fan-out, wafer bonding, or vertical stacking to improve bandwidth, density, and yield.
Vertical electrical connections etched through silicon dies or interposers for dense 3D and 2.5D chip integration.
Stacked DRAM memory using through-silicon vias and very wide interfaces for high-throughput GPUs and accelerators.
Processor design approach that composes multiple smaller dies into one package to improve yield, scaling, and product reuse.
Highly parallel processors originally built for graphics and later adapted for general-purpose and AI workloads.
Specialized processors optimized for matrix operations, tensor computation, and memory movement in machine-learning workloads.
Optical communication devices integrated with silicon processes to move data between chips, packages, and data-center systems.
